title: No Quarter November: How Is Doug Wilson Not an Abolitionist?
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman argues that five of Doug Wilson's foundational theological commitments (prophetic preaching, presuppositionalism, reformed providence, postmillennialism, and 'be faithful, not careful' boldness) lead inexorably to abolitionism, and that Wilson's ongoing defense of pro-life incrementalism contradicts his own stated theology. The piece also dismantles Wilson's 'local political,' 'strategic,' and 'pre-requisite' incrementalism categories.
audiences: Pastors and church leaders weighing abolitionist preaching
Abolitionist activists engaging Cross Politic listeners
Christians wary of nullification and federal backlash

questions.question: Why does Silberman believe Doug Wilson is uniquely positioned to slow the abolitionist movement compared to other pro-life voices?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists engaging Cross Politic listeners
questions.answer: Wilson's audience overlaps with the abolitionist base: conservative, reformed, postmillennial, theonomic, presuppositional Christians who would otherwise convert quickly to abolitionism but become the most belligerent opponents once Wilson has inoculated them against it.
questions.quote: He has the ability to slow the growth of the Abolitionist Movement and delay abolition in a way that no other pro-lifer does. The type of conservative, reformed, postmillennial, theonomic, presuppositionalist Christian who follows Doug Wilson is the type of person who is almost always quick to convert to abolitionism when presented with it. That is unless they’ve already come across Wilson’s anti-abolitionism, in which case they are the most belligerent anti-abolitionists around.

questions.question: Does the article require an abolitionist to share Wilson's reformed, postmillennial, and presuppositional commitments?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders weighing abolitionist preaching
questions.answer: No. Silberman explicitly notes the movement includes non-reformed, evidentialist, premillennial, and even dispensational abolitionists, and that the article's purpose is only to show how Wilson's particular doctrines should drive him toward abolitionism.
questions.quote: The purpose of this article is not to say that you have to be reformed, presuppositional, and/or postmillennial to be an abolitionist. It is merely to explain some of the ways these doctrines overlap with abolitionism and should lead a Christian toward the doctrines of abolitionism.

questions.question: What scriptural test does Silberman put to anyone defending incremental anti-abortion legislation?
questions.answer: He challenges them to find a single Old Testament prophet who called a society to gradual or piecemeal repentance, arguing the very idea is absurd and only seems plausible because politicians have habituated us to compromise.
questions.quote: I challenge Wilson or any pro-lifer trying to be Biblical to find one prophet who demanded a society turn from injustice gradually; one prophet from whose lips came a call for incremental repentance. Of course, this is a fool’s errand.

questions.question: How does Silberman frame abolitionism as the application of presuppositional apologetics?
questions.answer: He argues both disciplines insist on God's Word as the only coherent foundation, refuse pretended neutrality, name the underlying sin (unbelief or child sacrifice), and demand a total rather than partial repudiation of the opposing framework.
questions.quote: Abolitionism is applied presuppositionalism. Forget meddling with surface-level nonsense. Don’t trim the branches. Whether the sin be unbelief or child sacrifice, put the axe to the root.

questions.question: What does consistent reformed providence require of activists working to end abortion?
questions.answer: It requires trusting that God brings reformation through the uncompromising proclamation of His Word by His Church, treating obedience as the duty and leaving outcomes to God rather than imitating the calculating norms of worldly politicians.
questions.quote: We trust in God to providentially conquer His enemies through the uncompromising proclamation of His Word by His Church. Abolitionists are people of providence. Duty is ours. Results belong to God.

questions.question: How does Silberman accuse Wilson of inverting his own postmillennial preaching when the topic shifts to abortion?
questions.audience: Christians wary of nullification and federal backlash
questions.answer: Wilson normally preaches the conquering power of Christ over His enemies, but on abortion he switches to scaremongering about the federal government's might, behaving like the faithless Israelite spies who reported the land was full of giants.
questions.quote: But on abortion, Wilson speaks as an Israelite spy, reporting to the people of God that the supreme court has an army of Nephilim and that the Church is “like grasshoppers in their sight.”

questions.question: How does the article respond to fears that nullifying Roe will trigger federal violence?
questions.audience: Christians wary of nullification and federal backlash
questions.answer: Silberman judges economic retaliation as far likelier than physical conflict, insists abolitionism is non-violent, and says even violent persecution would be acceptable because the ongoing slaughter of preborn children already amounts to a civil war of comparable scale.
questions.quote: Conflict would only ever be initiated by the federal government as abolitionism is a peaceful movement, but if it comes to violent persecution against an abolitionist state, so be it. BABIES ARE BEING MURDERED. We refuse to value our lives more than theirs. We’re already in a civil war that takes as many lives as the first one every nine months.

questions.question: What charge does Silberman finally level against Wilson by appealing to Wilson's own Idaho sermon?
questions.answer: Wilson lauds Latimer, Ridley, and Bonhoeffer for refusing prudence, yet on the abortion holocaust he himself plays the cautious moderating voice he otherwise denounces.
questions.quote: Because when it comes to God’s image bearers in the womb being poisoned, suffocated, and ripped limb-from-limb, Doug Wilson is a careful man, and not a faithful one.

questions.question: How does the article distinguish 'incremental' progress from 'incrementalism' as a doctrine?
questions.answer: Immediatists also progress incrementally as more allies join, but the debate is over what is demanded: incrementalism advocates change by degrees in what is asked of government, while immediatism demands the immediate abolition of the evil itself.
questions.quote: Immediatists get closer to the goal incrementally as more people join the cause, more abolitionists get elected, more denominations and associations pass abolitionist resolutions, etc. The debate between incremental-“ism” and immediat-“ism” revolves around what we demand.

questions.question: Does Silberman concede any of Wilson's three categories of incrementalism?
questions.answer: He grants 'strategic incrementalism' (delaying a bill for tactical reasons can be wise) and partly grants 'pre-requisite incrementalism' (the gospel is foundational to social change), but rejects 'local political incrementalism' because abolishing abortion in any jurisdiction is obedience to Christ rather than degree-by-degree compromise.
questions.quote: Delaying invasion a few days to provide the best chance of success is not sin. Telling people when, where, and how to murder babies is.

questions.question: What does Silberman say is the actual definition of becoming 'carnal' in politics?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders weighing abolitionist preaching
questions.answer: Carnality is supporting compromise laws that regulate child sacrifice on the grounds that such regulation is the best politically attainable outcome, which substitutes worldly pragmatism for obedience to God's Word.
questions.quote: Supporting compromise laws that regulate child sacrifice because “it’s the best we can get” is a definition of becoming carnal.

questions.question: According to the C.R. Cali quotation Silberman cites, how should Christians measure success in fighting abortion?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders weighing abolitionist preaching
questions.answer: Success is measured by obedience to God's Word, not by the carnal results of how many babies are saved through unjust regulatory laws, even though saving babies is a godly desire.
questions.quote: But for the Christian, our primary objective is obedience. Our success is measured, not in the carnal results (in this case numbers of babies saved from being killed by their parents), but in whether or not we were obedient.

questions.question: What outcome does the article hope for if Wilson and his Cross Politic colleagues become abolitionists?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists engaging Cross Politic listeners
questions.answer: Silberman believes the kingdom impact would be 'immeasurable' and asks readers to pray that Wilson, Toby Sumpter, Chocolate Knox, and Gabe Rench would set aside personal distastes and follow their own theology to its conclusion.
questions.quote: If Doug Wilson, Toby Sumpter, Chocolate Knox, and Gabe Rench consistently applied their foundational theological beliefs to the abortion holocaust, they would be abolitionists. And were they to drop personal distastes, set aside historical biases, and follow God’s Word and their own theology to its unavoidable, logical conclusion of abolitionism, the impact for the kingdom of God and pushing back of darkness would be immeasurable.
